# Loredex dogfooding findings — nimbus-backend

## 2026-07-09 — Backend & Integrations developer (SSE streaming handoff)

Task: consume the nimbus-ai-engine token-streaming handoff, expose it as a
public SSE endpoint, update the API contract (openapi.yaml + Postman), and
hand off to nimbus-frontend.

### What worked well

- `loredex handoffs` as a session-start habit is genuinely good: one command
  surfaced the open handoff with objective and the exact vault path to the
  brief. It also pulls the remote first, so I never wondered if I was stale.
- The LLM-written inbound brief was high quality. It carried the chunk event
  shape (`{"type":"token"|"done","text":...}`), the "done is authoritative,
  not the concatenation" rule, and pointed at the exact section of the design
  note to read. I could have implemented from the brief alone; I opened the
  ai-engine note only for flush-cadence numbers (~40ms/~24 chars) and the
  "done always emitted after error" guarantee. Brief ~90% sufficient,
  source note needed for the last 10% of precision.
- The outbound LLM handoff (`handoff --to nimbus-frontend -y`) completed
  quickly and produced an excellent brief: endpoint, event semantics, two
  real gotchas, next actions, and it propagated my contract-ownership caveat.
  It auto-synced to the remote — no separate push step to forget.
- `route` picked up docs/streaming-api.md instantly and stamped it; `status`
  confirming "unrouted candidates: 0" is a nice done-signal.

### Friction

- The consume footer inside every handoff brief says
  `npx -y loredex@latest handoffs --consume ...` — a different invocation
  than the environment-configured one. In a team with a pinned config dir
  this is actively misleading; the footer should be config-aware or generic.
- `handoffs --consume` output is a single line. It doesn't say what changed
  (where did status flip?), whether the sender is notified, or whether the
  status change was pushed. Consumption is invisible to the producer.
- `route` says "routed 1 note(s)" but not where the note landed (project/
  topic folder, final filename). I had to `find` the vault to confirm
  filenames for wiki-links and reading order.
- Nothing links the handoff to git artifacts. My work is commit `f3a398e`
  (would be a PR in real life); the brief mentions the commit only because
  the LLM copied it from my note. There is no first-class field for
  "implements handoff X" on the commit or "implemented by PR Y" on a handoff.
- Checking whether the earlier consume + routed note reached teammates
  required a separate `sync` plus reasoning about git; there is no "what have
  I shipped to the vault this session" view.
- The API-contract gap (my beat): openapi.yaml and postman_collection.json
  live in this repo as source of truth, but the handoff system moves prose,
  not contract diffs. The frontend brief restates the endpoint in English;
  if the LLM had paraphrased a field name wrong, frontend would build against
  a hallucinated contract. Nothing validates brief-vs-openapi.

### What a native desktop app should do here

- Handoff inbox as a first-class pane: open handoffs with status, age,
  sender, one-click "open brief + linked notes", and a consume button that
  shows the resulting status change and notifies the producer.
- Producer-side handoff tracking: "you handed off to nimbus-frontend at
  14:02 — unconsumed for N days" instead of silence after `handoff written`.
- Contract diff viewer: when a handoff's source notes touch openapi.yaml /
  Postman, attach the machine-readable diff (endpoint added/changed, params,
  response schema) alongside the prose brief, rendered as a before/after
  contract view. An endpoint registry per handoff ("this handoff introduces
  GET /conversations/{id}/stream") would let consumers subscribe to
  endpoints, not repos.
- GitHub panel per handoff: linked branch/PR/commits on both ends — the
  producer's implementing PR and the consumer's consuming PR — so "who
  consumed what, with which code" is answerable without leaving the app.
- Route feedback: after filing a note, show where it landed in the vault
  tree and what wiki-links now point at it.
